data.description:
FIBRA Prologis Declares Quarterly Distribution

FIBRA Prologis Declares Quarterly Distribution

PR Newswire

MEXICO CITY, Feb. 16, 2026

MEXICO CITY, Feb. 16, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- FIBRA Prologis (BMV:FIBRAPL 14), a leading owner and operator of Class-A industrial real estate in Mexico, declared today a cash distribution of Ps. 1,262.1 million (US$73.2 million), or Ps. 0.7566 per Certificado Bursátil Fiduciario Inmobiliario ("CBFI") (US$ 0.0439 per CBFI).

The distribution is payable February 25, 2026, to CBFI holders.

Ex-dividend date of February 24, 2026.

Record date of February 24, 2026.

ABOUT FIBRA PROLOGIS

FIBRA Prologis is a leading owner and operator of Class-A industrial real estate in Mexico. As of September 30, 2025, the company's portfolio comprised 515 Investment Properties, totaling 87.0 million square feet (8.1 million square meters). This includes 348 logistics and manufacturing facilities across 6 industrial core markets in Mexico, comprising 65.7 million square feet (6.1 million square meters) of Gross Leasing Area (GLA) and 167 buildings with 21.3 million square feet (2.0 million square meters) of non-strategic assets in other markets.

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

The statements in this release that are not historical facts are forward-looking statements. These forward-looking statements are based on current expectations, estimates and projections about the industry and markets in which FIBRA Prologis operates, management's beliefs and assumptions made by management.  Such statements involve uncertainties that could significantly impact FIBRA Prologis financial results. Words such as "expects," "anticipates," "intends," "plans," "believes," "seeks," "estimates," variations of such words and similar expressions are intended to identify such forward-looking statements, which generally are not historical in nature.  All statements that address operating performance, events or developments that we expect or anticipate will occur in the future — including statements relating to rent and occupancy growth, acquisition activity, development activity, disposition activity, general conditions in the geographic areas where we operate, our debt and financial position, are forward-looking statements. These statements are not guarantees of future performance and involve certain risks, uncertainties and assumptions that are difficult to predict. Although we believe the expectations reflected in any forward-looking statements are based on reasonable assumptions, we can give no assurance that our expectations will be attained and therefore, actual outcomes and results may differ materially from what is expressed or forecasted in such forward-looking statements. Some of the factors that may affect outcomes and results include, but are not limited to: (i) national, international, regional and local economic climates, (ii) changes in financial markets, interest rates and foreign currency exchange rates, (iii) increased or unanticipated competition for our properties, (iv) risks associated with acquisitions, dispositions and development of properties, (v) maintenance of real estate investment trust ("FIBRA") status and tax structuring, (vi) availability of financing and capital, the levels of debt that we maintain and our credit ratings, (vii) risks related to our investments (viii) environmental uncertainties, including risks of natural disasters, (ix) risks related to the coronavirus pandemic, and (x) those additional factors discussed in reports filed with the "Comisión Nacional Bancaria y de Valores" and  the Mexican Stock Exchange by FIBRA Prologis under the heading "Risk Factors." FIBRA Prologis undertakes no duty to update any forward-looking statements appearing in this release.

Non-Solicitation - Any securities discussed herein or in the accompanying presentations, if any, have not been registered under the Securities Act of 1933 or the securities laws of any state and may not be offered or sold in the United States absent registration or an applicable exemption from the registration requirements under the Securities Act and any applicable state securities laws. Any such announcement does not constitute an offer to sell or the solicitation of an offer to buy the securities discussed herein or in the presentations, if and as applicable.

data.title: CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) Investors: March 13, 2026 Filing Deadline in Securities Class Action - Contact Kessler Topaz Meltzer & Check, LLP
data.description:
CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) Investors: March 13, 2026 Filing Deadline in Securities Class Action - Contact Kessler Topaz Meltzer & Check, LLP

CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) Investors: March 13, 2026 Filing Deadline in Securities Class Action - Contact Kessler Topaz Meltzer & Check, LLP

PR Newswire

RADNOR, Pa., Feb. 16, 2026

Were you affected by investment losses in CRWV securities between March 28, 2025, and December 15, 2025?

Affected Investor Losses Summary

  • CoreWeave, Inc. securities fraud class action filed
  • Purchasers or acquirers of CoreWeave, Inc. (NASDAQ: CRWV) securities
  • Seeking recovery of investment losses for material misstatements and/or omissions (as alleged) from March 28, 2025 through December 15, 2025
  • Kessler Topaz Meltzer & Check, LLP (www.ktmc.com) can assist at no cost to investor

RADNOR, Pa., Feb. 16, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- The law firm of Kessler Topaz Meltzer & Check, LLP (www.ktmc.com) informs investors that a securities fraud class action lawsuit has been filed against CoreWeave, Inc. ("CoreWeave") (NASDAQ: CRWV) on behalf of those who purchased or otherwise acquired CoreWeave securities between March 28, 2025, and December 15, 2025, inclusive (the "Class Period"). The lead plaintiff deadline is March 13, 2026.
  • Action: Securities fraud class action lawsuit filed
  • Company: CoreWeave, Inc. (NASDAQ: CRWV)
  • Affected investors: Purchasers or acquirers of CoreWeave, Inc. securities
  • Class Period: March 28, 2025 through December 15, 2025
  • Allegations: Material misstatements and/or omissions (as alleged)
  • Relief sought: Recovery of investment losses under the federal securities laws

The complaint alleges that, throughout the Class Period, Defendants made false and/or misleading statements and/or failed to disclose that: (1) CoreWeave had overstated the company's ability to meet customer demand for its service; (2) CoreWeave materially understated the scope and severity of the risk that CoreWeave's reliance on a single third-party data center supplier presented for the company's ability to meet customer demand for its services; (3) the foregoing was reasonably likely to have a material negative impact on CoreWeave's revenue; (4) as a result, CoreWeave's public statements were materially false and misleading at all relevant times.

CONTACT KESSLER TOPAZ MELTZER & CHECK, LLP (KTMC):
If you suffered CoreWeave losses, contact Kessler Topaz Meltzer & Check, LLP (KTMC) at:

https://www.ktmc.com/crwv-coreweave-inc-class-action-lawsuit?utm_source=PR_Newswire&utm_medium=pressrelease&utm_campaign=crwv&mktm=PR

You can also contact attorney Jonathan Naji, Esq. by calling (484) 270-1453 or by email at info@ktmc.com.

Learn more about CoreWeave, Inc. on YouTube:

  • CoreWeave, Inc. Securities Class Action Lawsuit (long video)
  • CoreWeave, Inc. Securities Class Action Lawsuit (short video)

THE LEAD PLAINTIFF PROCESS:
CoreWeave investors may, no later than March 13, 2026, seek to be appointed as a lead plaintiff representative of the class through Kessler Topaz Meltzer & Check, LLP or other counsel, or may choose to do nothing and remain an absent class member. A lead plaintiff is a representative party who acts on behalf of all class members in directing the litigation. The lead plaintiff is usually the investor or small group of investors who have the largest financial interest and who are also adequate and typical of the proposed class of investors. The lead plaintiff selects counsel to represent the lead plaintiff and the class and these attorneys, if approved by the court, are lead or class counsel. Your ability to share in any recovery is not affected by the decision of whether or not to serve as a lead plaintiff.

Kessler Topaz Meltzer & Check, LLP encourages CoreWeave investors who have suffered significant losses to contact the firm directly to acquire more information.

ABOUT KESSLER TOPAZ MELTZER & CHECK, LLP (KTMC):
Kessler Topaz Meltzer & Check, LLP (KTMC) is a leading U.S. plaintiff-side law firm focused on securities-fraud class actions and global investor protection. The firm represents individual investors as well as institutions, such as major pension funds, asset managers, and international investors. KTMC has led some of the largest recoveries in securities litigation and has been recognized by peers and the legal media with numerous accolades, including The National Law Journal's Plaintiff's Hot List and Trailblazers in Plaintiffs' Law, BTI Consulting Group's Honor Roll of Most Feared Law Firms, The Legal Intelligencer's Class Action Firm of the Year, Lawdragon's Leading Plaintiff Financial Lawyers, and Law360's Titans of the Plaintiffs Bar. The firm operates globally with offices in Pennsylvania and California. For more information about Kessler Topaz Meltzer & Check, LLP, please visit www.ktmc.com. 

CONTACT:
Kessler Topaz Meltzer & Check, LLP
Jonathan Naji, Esq.
(484) 270-1453
280 King of Prussia Road
Radnor, PA 19087
info@ktmc.com 

May be considered attorney advertising in certain jurisdictions. Past results do not guarantee future outcomes.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 5 PM ET: In a Chaotic Market, Investors Learn How to Cope With Surprises | The ...
data.description:
In a Chaotic Market, Investors Learn How to Cope With Surprises

One year into the second Trump administration, traders and investors say they are getting used to "event risk," outsize market swings triggered by unexpected news.

----

The shale boom that made the U.S. the world's top oil producer is nearing a crucial turning point

The shale-oil revolution that transformed the U.S. into the world's top oil producer is entering a new phase - one that could see America's hard-fought lead in energy erode in fewer than five years as oil production growth peters out.

----

The Break Is Over. Companies Are Jacking Up Prices Again.

Higher tariffs, labor and health-insurance costs have pushed many businesses to raise prices for consumers.

----

The Economy May Have Stuck the Soft Landing. Nobody Wants to Jinx It.

Inflation is easing, jobs are holding up, and growth is solid. But after years of high prices and with new risks emerging, declarations of victory feel premature.

----

Eurozone Industry Stumbles, But Outlook Remains Bright

Industrial production fell 1.4% in December, halting three consecutive months of expansion after output declined in Germany.

----

Japan's Narrow Growth Tests Fiscal, Monetary Policy Paths

The Japanese economy narrowly âreturned to growth in the final quarter of 2025, largely defying the U.S. tariff hit and giving the central bank room to keep raising rates.

----

Canada Factory Sales Inch Up

Factory shipments rose 0.6% on month in December, driven by sales of motor vehicles and food products.

----

Swiss Economy Swings to Growth

The economy grew 0.2% in the final quarter of 2025, shrugging off some of the U.S. tariff-related impact that hit activity earlier in the year.

----

Trump's Team Considers Overhaul to Steel and Aluminum Tariffs

The administration is weighing a plan to ease tariffs on some consumer goods while protecting U.S. companies facing overseas competition.

----

China Deploys a 'National Team' of Investors to Keep AI Stock Boom in Check

A group of state-linked funds has stepped in and unloaded holdings to cool things down when investors get too rosy.

----

Canada to Prioritize Domestic Firms in Defense-Spending Plan Amid U.S. Tension

Canada is seeking to create 125,000 jobs and increase corporate revenue by billions annually by ensuring domestic firms receive priority from a sizable increase in defense spending.

----

Inflation Slowed to 2.4% in January, Helped by Lower âGasoline Prices

While the reading was cooler than expected, consumers still saw higher costs for computers, âappliancesâ and hospital care. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  February 16, 2026 17:15 ET (22:15 GMT)

data.title: BHP 1st Half Net Profit, Dividend Rise; Signs $4.3 Billion Silver Streaming Deal
data.description:
By Rhiannon Hoyle 




  BHP Group on Tuesday reported a 28% rise in first-half net profit and raised its midyear dividend by nearly half, as the world's biggest miner by market value benefited from a record-breaking rally in copper prices. 



  The miner also said it has signed a $4.3 billion streaming deal for silver from the Antamina mine in Peru with Wheaton Precious Metals and aims to generate a total of up to $10 billion from similar deals, including a $2 billion power infrastructure agreement inked in December. 



  Melbourne, Australia-based BHP said it made a net profit of $5.64 billion for the six months through December, up from $4.42 billion in the year-earlier period. 



  Underlying profit, a closely watched measure of profitability that strips out some one-time charges, rose by 22% to $6.20 billion. BHP's underlying profit beat a consensus estimate of $6.03 billion compiled by Visible Alpha. 



  Directors of the company declared an interim dividend of 73 U.S. cents a share, up from 50 cents a share a year ago. 



  That equates to a payout of 60% of underlying profit, said BHP, which pledges to pay shareholders at least 50% of underlying attributable profit each reporting period. A year ago, it paid the equivalent of 50%. 



  The dividend reflects "strong operating performance, disciplined capital allocation and confidence in our outlook," said Chief Executive Mike Henry. 





  Write to Rhiannon Hoyle at rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  February 16, 2026 17:17 ET (22:17 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: In HelloNation, Insurance Expert Scott Hopson Explains Flood Zones and Insurance Requirements
data.description:
In HelloNation, Insurance Expert Scott Hopson Explains Flood Zones and Insurance Requirements

In HelloNation, Insurance Expert Scott Hopson Explains Flood Zones and Insurance Requirements

PR Newswire

PONTE VEDRA BEACH, Fla., Feb. 16, 2026

PONTE VEDRA BEACH, Fla., Feb. 16, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- What do homeowners in Ponte Vedra Beach need to know about how flood zones affect their insurance? A HelloNation article answers this question by breaking down the relationship between flood zones, policy coverage, and common misunderstandings that can lead to costly gaps in protection.

The article explains that many homeowners in the area mistakenly assume that only coastal properties face flood risk. However, the piece points out that flooding in Northeast Florida often results from factors like heavy rainfall, soil saturation, and poor drainage systems, not just proximity to the ocean. Even properties located far inland may experience significant water damage during severe storms, making the topic of flood zones relevant for nearly all residents.

According to the article, flood zones are determined using FEMA's Flood Insurance Rate Maps, which assess the probability of flooding during extreme weather events. High-risk zones are typically labeled as A or V, while areas considered moderate or low risk fall into X zones. The article stresses that living in an X zone does not eliminate risk, only reduces its statistical likelihood. Regardless of location, understanding flood insurance requirements helps homeowners avoid incorrect assumptions about what their policies will cover.

Homeowners with federally backed mortgages are required to carry flood insurance if their property lies within a high-risk zone. The HelloNation article highlights that this mandate applies even to homes that have never flooded before. Because FEMA updates these maps regularly, homeowners are advised to check their flood zone status periodically. The article suggests that working with a local insurance professional can help clarify whether a home is subject to flood insurance requirements in Ponte Vedra Beach.

Another key point made in the article is that homeowners' insurance Florida policies do not cover damage caused by rising water, storm surge, or surface flooding. This often surprises homeowners who have not reviewed their coverage in detail. Separate flood insurance is required to cover this type of loss. The article notes that reviewing both homeowners' and flood policies together is the best way to understand where one policy ends and another begins.

Even when flood insurance isn't required, the article explains that homes in lower-risk areas may still benefit from a flood insurance policy. These flood insurance Ponte Vedra Beach policies often have lower premiums and can prevent major financial loss in the event of an unexpected storm. Just a few inches of water, the article notes, can result in extensive and expensive damage.

The article also draws attention to the timing of coverage. Most flood insurance policies include a waiting period before coverage becomes active. Buying a policy shortly before a storm is forecast will likely be too late. Planning ahead is essential, especially before hurricane season in Florida begins. The article encourages early action to help avoid uncovered losses during high-risk months.

Policyholders are also advised to review coverage limits carefully. Flood insurance often covers the structure and, optionally, personal belongings, but limits may not reflect current replacement costs. The article emphasizes that this is one more reason to evaluate all insurance decisions, including the insurer's rating of a company. An insurer's rating reflects its ability to pay claims during widespread disasters, which can directly affect recovery efforts after a storm.

The article wraps up by encouraging homeowners to take a comprehensive look at their flood exposure well before hurricane season in Florida begins. Combining information about flood zones, flood insurance requirements, and insurer ratings helps homeowners form a clearer view of their actual risk and available protections. According to the article, informed preparation is key to maintaining both financial stability and peace of mind.

"What Homeowners In Ponte Vedra Should Know About Flood Zones & Insurance Requirements" features insights from Scott Hopson, Insurance Expert of Ponte Vedra Beach, FL, in HelloNation.

About HelloNation
HelloNation is a premier media platform that connects readers with trusted professionals and businesses across various industries. Through its innovative "edvertising" approach that blends educational content and storytelling, HelloNation delivers expert-driven articles that inform, inspire, and empower. Covering topics from home improvement and health to business strategy and lifestyle, HelloNation highlights leaders making a meaningful impact in their communities.

data.title: Sturm, Ruger & Company, Inc. to Report Fourth Quarter and Year-End 2025 Financial Results on Monday, March 2
data.description:
Sturm, Ruger & Company, Inc. to Report Fourth Quarter and Year-End 2025 Financial Results on Monday, March 2

Sturm, Ruger & Company, Inc. (NYSE: RGR) will announce its financial results for the fourth quarter and year-end 2025 and file its Annual Report on Form 10-K on Monday, March 2, 2026, after the close of the stock market.

That evening, Sturm, Ruger will host a webcast at 4:30 p.m. ET to discuss the fourth quarter and year-end 2025 operating results. Interested parties can listen to the webcast via this link or by visiting Ruger.com/corporate. Those who wish to ask questions during the webcast will need to pre-register prior to the meeting.

About Sturm, Ruger & Co., Inc.

Sturm, Ruger & Co., Inc. is one of the nation's leading manufacturers of rugged, reliable firearms for the commercial sporting market. With products made in America, Ruger offers consumers almost 800 variations of more than 40 product lines, across the Ruger, Marlin and Glenfield brands. For over 75 years, Sturm, Ruger & Co., Inc. has been a model of corporate and community responsibility. Our motto, "Arms Makers for Responsible Citizens®," echoes our commitment to these principles as we work hard to deliver quality and innovative firearms.

The Company may, from time to time, make forward-looking statements and projections concerning future expectations. data.title: In HelloNation, Real Estate Expert Lizzy Conroy of McLean, VA Shares Expert Tips for Selling a High-End Home in D.C. and Northern Virginia
data.description:
In HelloNation, Real Estate Expert Lizzy Conroy of McLean, VA Shares Expert Tips for Selling a High-End Home in D.C. and Northern Virginia

In HelloNation, Real Estate Expert Lizzy Conroy of McLean, VA Shares Expert Tips for Selling a High-End Home in D.C. and Northern Virginia

PR Newswire

MCLEAN, Va., Feb. 16, 2026

MCLEAN, Va., Feb. 16, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- What separates a successful luxury home sale from one that lingers on the market? According to a HelloNation article, Lizzy Conroy of HBC Group at SERHANT. explains that precision, presentation, and timing define results in the competitive world of D.C. real estate and Northern Virginia homes. The piece highlights how thoughtful pricing strategy, detailed luxury property preparation, and flawless staging create meaningful value and attract discerning buyers who expect excellence.

Conroy's approach begins with accurate pricing, a cornerstone of any luxury home sale. In the high-end segment, pricing strategy must balance analysis and instinct. Instead of relying on emotion, top agents focus on comparable sales, neighborhood trends, and unique design elements to set a competitive yet credible price. The HelloNation article explains that overpricing can cause hesitation among potential buyers, while strategic pricing signals confidence and builds trust. For D.C. real estate and Northern Virginia homes, establishing the right number early sets the tone for the entire selling process.

Once pricing is established, presentation becomes the key factor that elevates a listing from good to exceptional. The article describes how luxury property preparation involves transforming a house into an immersive experience for buyers. Staging plays a crucial role here; each room must feel balanced, intentional, and welcoming. Through the use of neutral colors, soft textures, and careful furniture placement, sellers can highlight architectural strengths while helping potential buyers envision themselves in the space. As Conroy notes, the goal is not just to showcase a home, but to tell its story visually and emotionally.

Visual presentation continues through professional photography and video marketing. Because most buyers begin their search online, high-quality imagery can make or break a luxury home sale. The HelloNation feature outlines how drone photography and cinematic video tours bring D.C. real estate and Northern Virginia homes to life, allowing prospective buyers to appreciate architectural flow, scale, and outdoor amenities. These visual tools transform a simple listing into an engaging representation of lifestyle, whether that means quiet mornings on a terrace or gatherings in a custom-designed living space.

Luxury property preparation often extends beyond furniture and photography. Conroy advises sellers to consider minor updates that subtly enhance value without requiring major renovations. Simple improvements, such as refinished floors, updated fixtures, or refreshed landscaping, signal care and modernity. Even small sensory details like lighting, scent, and background sound can influence perception, contributing to the overall presentation. This meticulous attention helps potential buyers view the property as both elegant and move-in ready, increasing its perceived value and appeal.

Timing remains another central element in every luxury home sale. The HelloNation article explains that spring and early fall typically mark peak periods in the D.C. real estate cycle, when properties look their best and buyers are most active. However, motivated clients often seek Northern Virginia homes year-round, especially in regions tied to government and international markets. Conroy's insights show that aligning marketing efforts with these cycles ensures maximum exposure and engagement, turning timing into a calculated advantage rather than a matter of chance.

Throughout the article, Conroy emphasizes that selling a high-end property requires more than simply listing it. The process integrates storytelling, data, and strategic visual presentation. An effective pricing strategy sets expectations; professional staging and luxury property preparation build emotional connection; and timing amplifies opportunity. Together, these steps create a cohesive selling experience that reflects not just a property's monetary worth, but its deeper, long-term value.

The HelloNation feature also underscores the importance of working with experienced real estate professionals who understand local nuances. In luxury home markets such as McLean, Georgetown, or Arlington, each neighborhood carries its own rhythm and buyer profile. Agents like Lizzy Conroy bridge these differences through market expertise and communication, ensuring that pricing, staging, and timing all align with current trends. This professional insight transforms complex sales into smooth, predictable outcomes.

Equally important, Conroy's perspective highlights the evolving expectations of today's luxury buyers. They seek homes that feel authentic, functional, and aligned with their lifestyles rather than simply grand. Sellers who adapt presentations to meet those expectations create stronger emotional resonance. According to the article, understanding what today's buyer values, efficiency, privacy, or architectural originality, enables sellers to craft a listing narrative that feels personal and compelling.

By focusing on presentation and pricing strategy, Conroy demonstrates how luxury home sales in D.C. and Northern Virginia can reach qualified buyers faster and more effectively. The process is about precision, not pressure. Sellers who combine accurate pricing, professional staging, and targeted timing are best positioned to highlight their home's full potential and achieve a sale that reflects its true worth.

Expert Tips for Selling a High-End Home in D.C. and Northern Virginia features insights from Lizzy Conroy, Real Estate Expert of McLean, Virginia, in HelloNation.

About HelloNation
HelloNation is a premier media platform that connects readers with trusted professionals and businesses across various industries. Through its innovative "edvertising" approach that blends educational content and storytelling, HelloNation delivers expert-driven articles that inform, inspire, and empower. Covering topics from home improvement and health to business strategy and lifestyle, HelloNation highlights leaders making a meaningful impact in their communities.
